verses.text: Moses went up from the plains of Moab unto the mountain of Nebo, to the top of Pisgah, over against Jericho. The LORD shewed him all the land — Gilead unto Dan, all Naphtali, Ephraim, Manasseh, all Judah unto the utmost sea, the south, the plain of Jericho, the city of palm trees, unto Zoar. The LORD said: This is the land which I sware unto Abraham, Isaac, Jacob: I have caused thee to see it with thine eyes, but thou shalt not go over thither. Guidance: A panoramic miracle — every tribe's portion in one view from Pisgah. God walks Moses through the deed before his death. The prophet who could not enter receives the highest pageantry: the LORD Himself as tour guide, the whole inheritance laid open as a farewell.

So Moses the servant of the LORD died there in the land of Moab, according to the word of the LORD. He buried him in a valley in the land of Moab, over against Beth-peor: but no man knoweth of his sepulchre unto this day. Guidance: God Himself buries Moses. No human hands, no funeral procession, no monument. Why? Lest Israel turn the grave into a shrine. The man who refused to be worshipped was hidden by the only One who could be worshipped. His epitaph in this verse: 'the servant of the LORD' — the title he wore his whole life.

No human hands, no funeral procession, no monument. Why? Lest Israel turn the grave into a shrine. The man who refused to be worshipped was hidden by the only One who could be worshipped. His epitaph in this verse: 'the servant of the LORD' — the title he wore his whole life.

Moses was an hundred and twenty years old when he died: his eye was not dim, nor his natural force abated. The children of Israel wept for Moses in the plains of Moab thirty days: so the days of weeping and mourning for Moses were ended. Guidance: 120 years, eyes still sharp, body still strong — Moses didn't die of old age; he died of completion. The mountain didn't wear him out; the assignment did. Thirty days of national weeping — the longest mourning in the Torah. The man who watched everyone else mourn finally got mourned.

Moses was an hundred and twenty years old when he died: his eye was not dim, nor his natural force abated. Guidance: 120 years, eyes still sharp, body still strong — Moses didn't die of old age; he died of completion. The mountain didn't wear him out; the assignment did. Thirty days of national weeping — the longest mourning in the Torah. The man who watched everyone else mourn finally got mourned.

Joshua son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom; for Moses had laid his hands upon him: the children of Israel hearkened unto him, did as the LORD commanded Moses. Guidance: Authority transferred. Hands laid on a head, spirit poured in, people hearken — succession works. The new leader stands fully equipped because the old leader did his last act in obedience: he blessed and laid hands. The kingdom is never an orphanage.

There arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face, in all the signs and wonders which the LORD sent him to do in the land of Egypt — to Pharaoh, to all his servants, to all his land — and in all that mighty hand, all the great terror which Moses shewed in the sight of all Israel. Guidance: The Torah closes with an epitaph the Spirit Himself signed off on. No prophet ever like him — until the One who would come as the prophet-like-Moses (Deut 18:18). Face to face with God; signs in Egypt; mighty hand. The book of Israel's beginnings ends with the man whose face still shone. The Pentateuch closes — the river still ahead, the promise still alive, Moses' bones in a grave nobody can find.
